Sarkar, Krishnendu
Devi, Pooja
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Kumar, Praveen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new carbon-based nanomaterials with unique electronic, optical, and physicochemical properties. Both CQDs and GQDs have received attention in different material research fields such as optoelectronics, energy harvesting, chemical sensing, and biosensing. The combination of edge effects and/or zero-dimensional quantum-confined structures make them a promising alternative for applications like LED emitters, photodetectors, solar cells, water splitting, and optoelectronic devices. Despite the great potential for using these materials in energy harvesters, their potential in energy applications has not yet been reviewed thoroughly. In this review, we particularly focused on the role of edge effects and attached functional groups on flexible optoelectronic devices for energy harvesting applications. In addition, we also discussed the underlying challenges and future prospects for CQD/GQD-based devices with respect to their performance, sustainability, durability, and cost-effectiveness to efficiently realize their industrial scale-up.

head.ce:title: Current and future perspectives of carbon and graphene quantum dots: From synthesis to strategy for building optoelectronic and energy devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new carbon-based nanomaterials with unique electronic, optical, and physicochemical properties. Both CQDs and GQDs have received attention in different material research fields such as optoelectronics, energy harvesting, chemical sensing, and biosensing. The combination of edge effects and/or zero-dimensional quantum-confined structures make them a promising alternative for applications like LED emitters, photodetectors, solar cells, water splitting, and optoelectronic devices. Despite the great potential for using these materials in energy harvesters, their potential in energy applications has not yet been reviewed thoroughly. In this review, we particularly focused on the role of edge effects and attached functional groups on flexible optoelectronic devices for energy harvesting applications. In addition, we also discussed the underlying challenges and future prospects for CQD/GQD-based devices with respect to their performance, sustainability, durability, and cost-effectiveness to efficiently realize their industrial scale-up.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs, CDs, or C-dots) are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with very small sizes (1–10 nm), whereas graphene quantum dots (GQDs or G-dots) are small fragments of graphene with extraordinary luminescence properties. The accidental discovery of CQDs from fragments of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Xu et al. in 2004 created this new class of carbon nanomaterials [1]. These visible fluorescent nanomaterials were referred as ‘‘carbon quantum dots’’ by Sun et al. [2] in 2006. Following the discovery of graphene, the confinement of one-dimensional (1D) graphene nanoribbon to zero-dimensional (0D) structures was demonstrated [3–5]. These 0D structures are referred as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and they exhibit very high opto-electronic properties. These CQDs/GQDs have advanced properties over conventional semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs). Further, they have many advantageous properties such as environmental friendliness (low toxicity), a facile synthesis procedure, cost-effectiveness, high sustainability, and advanced optical/electrical properties [6–9]. Due to these intrinsic properties, the feasibility of using CQDs/GQDs has been demonstrated from a myriad of applications in material sciences, chemical sciences, biological sciences, and physical sciences due to their many beneficial material properties (e.g., biocompatibility, photostability, chemical inertness, exceptional multi-photon excitation, and low cost) [10,11].

Considerable research efforts have been made on CQDs/GQDs, as reflected by the continuously increasing number of publications since 2000 (Fig. 1 a, Scopus database) and the total citations (Fig. 1b, Scopus database). These statistics clearly show that CQDs/GQDs are popular subjects of research (Fig. 1c, Scopus database for “Carbon dots and graphene quantum dots”: as of July 2020). Although several reviews have been made in the recent past, most of these efforts concentrated on the synthesis of CQDs/GQDs and applications in bio-sensing and bio-imaging for CQDs [12–21]. Hola et al. [16] described the potential application of CQDs in the fields of biomedicine and optoelectronics with synthesis strategies for cell imaging and targeted drug delivery. Li et al. [21] further reviewed the potential of these CQDs/GQDs for optoelectronic and energy devices. These authors have particularly emphasized their applications for light-emitting diodes (LEDs), photovoltaics, photodetectors, photocatalysis, batteries, and super-capacitors. The overall application perspectives of CQDs have also been described with respect to the effects of surface passivation and functionalization for the control of physicochemical properties. Likewise, the multifaceted roles of CQDs were described in their applications in photocatalysis [18], metal ion sensing [19], photodetectors [22–24], solar cells [25–27], LED emitters [28,29], flexible devices [30,31] and many other optoelectronic and energy applications [32]. Most of the reviews published for CQDs/GQDs focused on the chemical applications of the CQDs rather than on the energy sector, which may have fascinating futuristic applications for CQDs/GQDs [21].

In this review, we first discuss the material properties of CQDs/GQDs with respect to their chemical/electronic structure and induced defects in their structures. We then describe their applications in energy harvesting devices, in particular for photodetectors, solar cells, LED emitters, flexible/wearable electronic devices, and hydrogen fuel. We also discuss the advantages of CQDs/GQDs in the above applications over the pre-existing materials that have been leading the specific fields. The role of edge effects and functional groups are duly addressed. Our discussion is further extended to describe the underlying challenges and future directions. As discussed above, the CQDs/GQDs have potential to be used for futuristic large-scale device applications. Therefore, this topical and focused review is much needed for the scientific community working on the CQDs/GQDs related areas, especially towards energy harvesting devices.

CQDs have been synthesized using raw materials with very complex structures based on a variety of techniques. The synthesis of GQDs is straightforward relative to CQDs. In contrast, GQDs have a well-established graphene structure. Interestingly, almost every synthesis technique imparts some new properties that could help expand their applicability such as size-dependent photoluminescence (PL) [33–36], photo-blinking [37–39], excitation-dependent PL [40], hydrophilic surfaces and surface ligands [2]. Thus, it is very important to analyze each of these properties based on the very complex structure of CQDs coming out of different synthesis routes [41]. Graphene sheets were fragmented into blue luminescent GQDs via a hydrothermal route, and these are useful for optoelectronic applications [42]. In general, synthesis strategies are classified into two broad segments, top-down and bottom-up approaches, along with a new segment called a waste-derived route (Fig. 2 ).

In the top-down approach, CQDs/GQDs were prepared from bulk carbon, graphite powder, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO) or graphene nanoribbons (GNR) through a number of routes (e.g., arc-discharge methods, laser ablation, plasma reactors, ultrasonic synthesis, chemical exfoliation, and electrochemical oxidation). Herein, we briefly discuss a few of these available synthesis methods.

The discovery of CQDs was made unintentionally during the purification of arc-discharged single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) in 2004 by Hu et al. [1]. After purification, they found a new kind of fragmented nanoparticle along with short/tubular carbon and SWCNTs. Astonishingly, those carbon nanoparticles showed bright emission under 365 nm light excitation into a mixed band of blue, green, yellow, and orange. Quantitatively, these fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were found to constitute only 10% of the mass of the SWCNT, with a very low quantum yield of ~0.016 for yellow band emission. Elemental analysis indicated that these fluorescent nanoparticles contained no elements other than C, H, N, and O. Arc-discharge is a method to reorganize the carbon atoms decomposed from the bulk carbon precursors in the anodic electrode under gas plasma in a sealed reactor. Under high-energy plasma, the temperature in the reactor can reach as high as 4,000 K. Hence, the carbon vapor assembles to form CQDs in the cathode. CQDs prepared by arc discharge possess large oxygen contents to exhibit intense fluorescence without surface modification. However, CQDs generated by arc discharge generally exhibit complex compositions with impurities which are hard to remove and/or extract.

Laser ablation is well accepted as a sophisticated technique to prepare different nanostructured morphologies. Fig. 3 a presents a schematic of synthesized CQDs. Sun et al. first proposed laser ablation using a Nd:YAG laser source (1064 nm, 10 Hz) to a carbon target followed by acid treatment [2]. Initially, acid-washed CQDs did not emit any light. However, after surface modification by oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) and poly(propionylethyleneimine-coethyleneimine), a bright PL emission was observed (Fig. 3b and c).

Later, controlled synthesis of CQDs with different sizes was demonstrated by manipulating the nucleation via laser pulse width [43]. The high energy laser creates a localized plasma plume interacting with graphite flakes at the interface of the solid target and surrounding liquid medium (step 1 of Fig. 3a) to form a readily expandable bubble. After the laser pulse, the surrounding liquid pressure shrank the bubble, while the slowly cooling core initiated nucleation of the CQDs (Step 2 of Fig. 3a). CQDs with different cluster densities were generated with different laser pulse widths, which assisted in the formation of CQDs with different sizes (and unique properties).

Arc plasma reactors have been used by Denes et al. [44] in 2010 to synthesize CQDs. They demonstrated a one-step synthesis of functionalized CQDs using an arc plasma reactor (Fig. 3d). The mechanism involves the generation of free radicals by the helium plasma, which are actually responsible for CQDs synthesis. Here, ethylenediamine was used for the in-situ functionalization of the CQDs. This technique provided enhanced aqueous dispersibility of primary-amine functionalized CQDs with uniform sizes. A submerged-arc plasma reactor was built using a cylindrical Pyrex glass chamber as shown in Fig. 3d. Teflon caps were provided for the cavitation gas supply tubing and electrical connections. In an experimental synthesis of CQDs, benzene and helium were introduced into the reactor as source and cavitation gas, respectively. During discharge process, benzene turns instantly into black and get converted into CQDs.

Sonochemical synthesis routes involve intense ultrasound to design nanocarbon materials without long reaction times under high temperatures and pressures. Note that several factors such as chemical composition, size, shape, surface structure, solubility, and aggregation are influenced by ultrasonic production or modification of carbon nanomaterials. Changes in the nanomaterials are the consequences of acoustic cavity formation followed by the imploding and collapsing of the liquid bubbles as described in Fig. 3e [45]. Hydrodynamic cavitation is triggered by a strong local decrease in pressure due to supersonic liquid jets. This results in the formation of critical bubbles [46,47], which can further fragment macroscopic carbon materials into CQDs/GQDs. Jhuo et al. [47] synthesized GQDs using the ultrasonic technique with diameters of 3–5 nm, whereas Li et al. [46] showed that both carbon nanostructures and composite of CQDs/Cu2O could be designed by a facile one-step ultrasonic treatment. Kumar et al. [45] introduced a synthesis of hybrid Sn@CQDss@Sn anode via ultrasonic techniques.

GQDs/CQDs have been synthesized by electrochemical cleavage of carbon precursors such as carbon nanotubes, graphite rods, rGO film, and CVD-grown graphene. The mechanism of these GQDs/CQDs synthesis involved OH• and O• radicals formed from anodic oxidation of water. These radicals act as electrochemical ‘‘scissors’’ to produce CQDs/GQDs. Exfoliation is normally initiated at edge sites and accelerated at the defect sites. In case of organic solvents, the exfoliation process is governed by the electrical stress followed by the anion intercalation between adjacent layers of graphene. Note that the combustion is a simple tool to prepare CQDs. The raw materials required for combustion are easily available (e.g., candle ash, natural gas ash, and paraffin putty). Candle soot has been used to prepare both CQDs/GQDs via combustion and chemical exfoliation routes. For instance, multicolor fluorescent, water-soluble carbon nanoparticles ~2 nm size were obtained from the combustion soot of candles [48]. They first refluxed the candle soot with nitric acid followed by electrophoresis to purify the CQDs. Later, Ye et al. [49] showed that GQDs could be synthesized by exfoliating common (earth-abundant) coal. The macroscopic and nanoscale structure of coal is shown in Fig. 3f. Peng et al. [50] used the chemical exfoliation technique to prepare GQDs from the oxidative cutting of carbon fiber with sizes in the micrometer range (Fig. 3g). Oxidative acid treatment using combustion process to synthesize CQDs/GQDs has three important aspects: (a) fragmentation of the aggregates of CQDs/GQDs to obtain nano sized carbon structure, (b) provision of required surface functional groups for water solubility, and (c) modulation of the fluorescence properties for the CQDs/GQDs.

Electrochemical oxidation is the most widely used inexpensive and high yield technique for the preparation of CQDs/GQDs [51–53]. An electrochemical approach was employed for the first time to synthesize strong blue luminescent CQDs from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in 2007 by Zhou et al. [51]. The electrochemical synthesis of CQDs was performed in a degassed acetonitrile solution with the addition of 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) in a three-electrode cell with the following electrode combination: chemical vapor deposited MWCNT on carbon paper as a working electrode (WE), a Pt wire as counter electrode (CE), and an Ag/AgClO4 as reference electrode (RE). Later, Lu et al. [52] demonstrated a facile route to synthesize CQDs via ionic liquid-assisted electrochemical exfoliation. In an electrochemical cell, they used a graphite rod and highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) as an anode in two separate experiments along with Pt wire as a CE in an ionic liquid electrolyte. The ionic liquids used were 1-methyl-3-butylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate and 1-methyl-3-butylimidazolium chloride.

The bottom-up approach typically involves pyrolysis or carbonization of organic precursors as building blocks. Citric acid and saccharides are the common raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs/GQDs. These methods are very cost-effective, scalable, and it is easy to control the sizes of the dots based on the requirement. Among the bottom-up techniques, microwave, hydrothermal, thermal pyrolysis, and metal-organic framework (MOF) template-assisted options are commonly accepted, as briefly discussed below.

Microwave techniques involve electromagnetic waves in the range of 1 mm to 1 m that are capable of breaking chemical bonds to facilitate the preparation of new structures over a very short reaction time (2–10 min) [54–56]. Zhu et al. [54] were the first to report the microwave route to prepare CDs from poly(ethylene glycol) and saccharide (glucose, fructose) as starting blocks. On the other hand, Tang et al. [55] developed a unique microwave-assisted hydrothermal method to prepare GQDs from glucose (Fig. 4 a and b). The maximum photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of these GQDs were reported to be in the range of 7–11% when emitting white light under blue excitation. Zhai et al. [56] reported microwave pyrolysis to prepare high PLQY (~30%) materials using citric acid as a building block in which the CQDs surface was passivated by 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). They used different amine groups like diethylamine (DEA), triethylamine (TEA), and 1,4-butane diamine (BDA) for surface passivation to yield highly luminescent CQDs (Fig. 4c) [45].

Hydrothermal carbonization is an efficient, low-cost technique for synthesizing CQDs/GQDs from saccharides or organic acids [57–61]. Generally, organic precursors sealed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor are used at high-temperature (180–200 °C) to create high pressure inside the reactor [57] for the synthesis of CQDs/GQDs. These authors used l-ascorbic acid as a building block to prepare CQDs in a single step hydrothermal technique. On the other hand, Hsu et al. [59] discovered the formation of CQDs through a hydrothermal route. They showed that the growth model involves four steps: i) dehydration, ii) polymerization, iii) carbonization, and iv) passivation (Fig. 4d). Amino and carboxylic acid groups (i.e., 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-propane-1,3-diol or ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) were helpful in the formation of CQDs. In the hydrothermal technique, hydrogen bonds play a vital role during molecular assembly and heating to facilitate the polymerization and nucleation for CQDs growth. The synthesis of CQDs through a hydrothermal method was also reported using glucose and monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4) as precursors [58].

A thermal pyrolysis technique was developed for the synthesis of CQDs by Giannelis et al. [62]. These authors prepared carbogenic nanoparticles with surface functionalization via thermal carbonization of different ammonium citrate salts. The carbonization of the octadecyl ammonium (C18H37NH+ 3) citrate salt resulted in organophilic CQDs, whereas hydrophilic CQDs were prepared via carbonization of the 2-(2-aminoethoxy)-ethanol (OHCH2CH2OCH2CH2NH+ 3) salt. The thermal dehydration of the ammonium carboxylate moieties formed amide linkages (–NHCO–) between the organic corona and the cores. Later, they reported the formation mechanism of carbogenic CQDs through pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and ethanolamine (EA) under different temperatures [63]. They also demonstrated the strong dependence of the pyrolysis temperature of CQDs during synthesis on their resulting PL behaviors (e.g., in terms of intensity and emission). At the lower pyrolysis temperature of 180 °C, the PL spectrum was attributed to the dehydration of CA−EA. Whereas, at a pyrolysis temperature of 230 °C, they observed the presence of both molecular fluorophores and the carbogenic core, which together are responsible for the PL spectrum. Further, they established the PL emission from only carbogenic cores with an increase in pyrolysis temperature to 300 °C–400 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MOF template-assisted synthesis approach for CQDs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The well-defined pore sizes in MOFs serve as a template to synthesize uniform-sized CQDs due to the rich isoreticular pores that are 1–10 nm in size. MOF assisted synthesis of CQDs is a very recent approach initiated by Gu et al. in 2017 [64]. They used MOFs with three different pore diameters, HKUST-1 (largest at ~ 1.35 nm), ZIF-8 (pore diameter ~ 1.9 nm), and MIL-101 (pore diameter ~3.4 nm) as the host templates. In a typical synthesis procedure, MOF pores were filled with a solution of glucose in EtOH/H2O (9:1). Then, the MOF powder was dipped in that solution. Glucose started to decompose as the loaded MOF powder was heated to 200 °C. Here, the MOF template could sustain temperatures higher than 280 °C. The CQDs were collected from the pore of the template by washing in a KOH solution to dissolve the template (Fig. 4e). The controllability of the size and morphology provided by the MOF assisted synthesis encouraged other forms of templates to prepare highly luminescent CQDs/GQDs including: copolymer Pluronic P123 and mesoporous silica (OMS) SBA-15) [65–67].

Some CQDs/GQDs synthesis techniques were also developed by adopting the carbonization method of waste materials as carbon sources. In the waste-derived route, the source of carbon can be derived from various forms of waste resources (e.g., fruit and vegetable) [68–73]. This waste-derived synthesis of CQDs allowed us to use natural resources in an eco-friendly way. Zhou et al. [71] used watermelon peel as a source of carbon to synthesize highly fluorescent CQDs (Fig. 5 a). Sweet pepper has been used by Yin et al. [70] to prepare CQDs (Fig. 5b). Pomelo peel was used by Lu et al. [73] to synthesize highly fluorescent CQDs (PLQY ~6.9%) by hydrothermal processing (Fig. 5c). Pooja et al. have reported the synthesis of CQDs by using dairy waste, fruit waste, and expired drugs based on a one-step pyrolysis method [74]. Orange juice was also used by Sahu et al. [72], and the resulting materials showed a PLQY of ~26% (Fig. 5d). Among the vegetable sources, cabbage was used as a raw material for the synthesis of CQDs by Alam et al. [68] (Fig. 5e). After carefully washing the cabbage, it was mixed with ultrapure water and placed into a domestic fruit-juicer for further processing, followed by transfer to a hydrothermal reactor at 140 °C for 5 h. Finally, the obtained CQD-containing solution was dialyzed in Mili-Q water using a tubular dialysis membrane for 48 h.

Most of the reports reveal that CQDs consist of an amorphous carbogenic core and a large number of surface functional groups. In the majority of the reports, the synthesized CDQs were amorphous. In contrast, some reports showed the existence of crystalline sp2 carbon within the core [51,53]. On the other hand, GQDs showed very good crystallinity that stemmed from the graphene lattices inside the dots. Although CQDs and GQDs differ in the crystallinity, both surfaces have oxygen-containing functional groups.

Different chemical structures have been suggested for the CQD cores, such as amorphous carbon [75–77], a diamond-like crystal structure [40], and graphite or graphite oxide structures [62,78,79]. Zhu et al. [75] have synthesized CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine via a hydrothermal method. They observed a broad peak near 25° (0.34 nm) in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, which was correlated with disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 6 a). Bao et al. [79] synthesized crystalline core CQDs from carbon fibers via electrochemical etching in an electrolyte of 0.1 M TBAP in acetonitrile. They collected the CQDs by evaporating acetonitrile and re-dispersing the material in double-distilled water. The lattice spacing of the CQDs calculated by HRTEM was about 0.325 nm, corresponding to the (002) facet (Fig. 6b). Hu et al. [40] reported the laser-ablated synthesis of CQDs followed by their passivation with polyethylene glycol (PEG200 N) in water. The observed lattice spacing of these CQDs was reported to 0.20–0.23 nm. The TEM images of both PEG passivated (Fig. 6c) and non-passivated (Fig. 6d) CQDs and the corresponding SAED patterns revealed diamond-like crystallinity, as confirmed from the HRTEM images (Fig. 6e). These authors have demonstrated uniform distributions (Fig. 6f) of these CQDs with an average size of ~3 nm.

The electronic structures of GQDs are very close to that of a graphene structure, apart from the surface functional groups, while CQDs are mainly composed of organogenic elements and molecules. The electronic structures of CQDs could be explained by molecular orbital (MO) theory, which could be classified into three subsections, namely i) bonding, ii) anti-bonding, and iii) nonbonding [53,77,80,81]. Depending on the symmetry of rotation, orbitals in the ground state could further be classified as σ (symmetrical) and π (asymmetrical). A total of five MO types were assigned including: σ, π, n (nonbonding), σ* (anti-bonding), and π* (anti-bonding). Every possible transition had a specific absorption range in UV–vis spectroscopy with absorption ranges: σ/σ* at ~125 nm, n/σ* at 150–250 nm, π/π* at 200–400 nm, and the n/π* transition at 400–700 nm [80]. The π-states of CQDs originated from aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons in their core, whereas the π-states arose from the lone pair electron containing functional groups (e.g., carbonyls, amines, amides, and thiol). The n/π* transition took place when the functional groups containing electron lone pairs were attached to sp2-hybridized carbons. Further, these n/π* transitions had low dipole moments (10–100 L mol−1 cm−1), resulting in very low molar absorptivity.

CQDs prepared by top-down approaches possess larger numbers of defects than the bottom-up option, likely because of the possibly higher degree of surface oxidation. The greater extent of surface oxidation introduces more surface defects on the synthesized CQDs. These defects are referred to as molecular states and are responsible for the fluorescence of CQDs [82–85]. Different chemical groups such as a diamine, thiol [82], hydrazides [83], and alkylamine [84] have been functionalized on CQDs/GQDs. They generally act as electron-donating groups to enhance PLQY by preventing non-radiative recombination [84]. Ghosh et al. [85] reported the occurrence of XRD peak broadening in CQDs (compared to GQDs) because of the surface functional groups. They have observed the presence of D- and G-band peaks for GQDs in the Raman spectrum, whereas no signal was recorded for CQDs. This difference should be attributed to the effective presence of aliphatic and aromatic groups and the absence of graphitic domains in CQDs [85,86].

The optical absorption property of CQDs/GQDs has efficient applications in the photon harvesting in the short wave length area due to the π– π* transition of the aromatic CC bonds with a tail extending to the visible range [85]. The GQDs show comparatively weaker absorption where the maxima at~380 nm is due to n−π* transition of transition of CO bonds and oxygen containing functional groups. Normally the optical absorption of CQDs is comparatively higher than GQDs in the extended wave lengths. Although CQDs/GQDs had absorption peak in the range 260–380 nm, surface passivation and functional groups could be considered to modify the adsorption features.

Excellent photoluminescence (PL) property of CQDs/GQDs has been a key to their novel applications. Normally, the PL quantum yield (QY) obtained for bare CQDs has poor (~10–15%) values, because of the traps on the surface. Surface passivation or modification dramatically enhanced the QY. The superior PL features also comes from the quantum confinement of CQDs/GQDs. However, GQDs show greater QY property than CQDs due to the presence of layers in their structure and improved crystallinity. The luminescence mechanism in CQDs/GQDs is also the most challenging issues to require more in-depth researches. The main advantage of CQDs is excellent photo-stability with non-blinking PL. The non-blinking PL ability enables single-molecule tracking while the photo-stability is responsible for long-term real-time imaging. The absorption property of the CQDs made it very useful for the solar energy harvesting. Apart from PL, phosphorescence based on water-soluble CQDs has also been introduced with phosphorescent duration in the order of sub-second [87]. It was suggested that the phosphorescence should originate from the triplet-excited states of aromatic carbonyls on the surface of the CQDs.

Electrical property of CQDs is analogous to graphene oxide (GO) sheets when it carries oxygenated functional groups. In contrast, GQDs are a few nanometer-sized GO with oxygenated functional groups. The oxygen containing functional groups are responsible for disordering of the conductive sp2 carbon network at the basal plane which facilitates the electron transfer. CQDs/GQDs have large number of surface functional groups which directly influences the electrical conductivity. The correlation between functionalization and electrical properties could have electrical attributes from: (a) large edge states and quantum confinement via structural constraints, (b) change in the hybridization state of carbon atoms, (c) creation of a barrier at the functionalization site and within the electron-potential continuum, and (d) introduction of new energy levels via functionalization [88–90].

The basic principle of a photodetector is to convert incident light into an electrical signal. Generally, photodetectors (PDs) can be classified into two types depending on the photocurrent generation mechanisms. The first type is the photocurrent generated due to the excitation of free carriers from optical transitions such as the photovoltaic effect, photoconductive effect, and photogating effect. The second type is due to thermal, photothermoelectric (PTE), and bolometric effects. The basic mechanism involved in the photovoltaic effect is the creation of a built-in potential at the junction, which helps to separate the photogenerated electrons and holes. In contrast, in the photoconductive technique, photoinduced carriers increased the free carrier concentration to suppress the resistance of the detector circuit. The photothermoelectric effect is the temperature gradient induced by light irradiation, which generates a thermoelectric voltage at the semiconductor channel that drives current through the device.

The performance of photodetectors under different operating conditions is measured using a set of performance metrics like responsivity (R), specific detectivity (D*), external quantum efficiency (EQE), response time, and photoconductive gain (G). Photoresponsivity is defined as the ratio of the photocurrent to the incident light power, whereas the external quantum efficiency is the number of collected charge carriers per incident photon. The responsivity (R) is defined as R = JPh/Plight, where JPh and Plight correspond to the photocurrent density and input light intensity, respectively. Specific detectivity represents the noise in the PD signal defined as D = R/√(2qJd), where q and Jd represent the electronic charge and dark current density, respectively. The response time of the PDs includes the rise time (τr) and decay/fall time (τf). Rise time (τr) is the time taken by the PDs to reach 90% of the saturation photocurrent, whereas fall time (τf) can be defined as the time required to drop down to 10% from the saturated photocurrent.

CQD/GQD-based photodetectors can be classified into three regions based on the spectrum response wavelength: UV detectors, visible light detectors, and near mid-infrared detectors [91–94]. Commonly, π–π* transitions for both CQDs/GQDs result in absorption in the deep-UV region (absorption peak ~250 nm). As a result, deep-UV photodetectors are mostly reported with CQDs/GQDs [85,95]. Nonetheless, when it comes to photodetection, GQDs [96–100] have an advantage over CQDs due to better electrical conductivity and crystallinity [85].

UV photodetectors can be used in communications and medical tools. As such, biocompatible and environmentally-friendly CQD/GQD-based UV detectors have become more popular than other oxide and nitride-based UV detectors [101–103]. Zhang et al. first reported deep UV (DUV) photodetectors using GQDs in photoconductive mode [95]. They showed that the device performance was greatly influenced by the choice of electrode structure (i.e., symmetric or asymmetric form) (Fig. 7 ).

The device was fabricated by drop-casting GQDs on a quartz substrate with a previously patterned electrode. The effects of symmetric (Au/Au and Ag/Ag) and asymmetric (Ag/Au) electrodes were investigated (Fig. 7a–d). The photoresponse was very poor when measured in air. In contrast, a significant increase in the photocurrent was observed when tested in a vacuum (Fig. 7b). The reason for this is that in air, GQDs adsorbed oxygen as well as water molecules on their hydrophilic surfaces, resulting in the accumulation of holes on the GQD surface. Under UV illumination, photogenerated electrons were captured by the accumulated holes, desorbing the oxygen moieties, which resulted in the low photoresponse. Under vacuum conditions, the GQD surface does not have oxygen-containing groups, and they tend to exhibit fewer defects. When they are illuminated with UV light, the photogenerated electrons and holes can be collected very efficiently without considerable loss to the defect states. Hence, GQD-based PDs can manifest a higher response in vacuum conditions.

It was interesting to observe that the device with the asymmetric electrode structure exhibited a strongly recognizable rectification (~20 at 5 V) under UV light with a high on/off ratio of ~6000 under 254 nm illumination. The mechanism of the symmetric and asymmetric electrode could be understood from the band diagrams (Fig. 8 ). Au with a high work function (5.1 eV) created a large Schottky barrier at the Au/GQDs junction (Fig. 8a) so that the gradient helped electrons flow towards the Au anode, while the hole transfer is hindered by the existence of the potential barrier at the Au cathode. In contrast, Ag promoted holes flow towards another electrode due to its comparatively low work function (4.26 eV). It also restricted electron flow due to the presence of a small barrier, as described in Fig. 8b. Hence, in the asymmetric electrode configuration, photogenerated electrons can flow towards the Au cathode, while the holes can transfer freely toward the Ag anode without facing any barriers (Fig. 8c).

UV detectors based on high bandgap oxide materials have tremendous potential due to a larger exciton binding energy of 60 meV and a high carrier mobility [103,104]. Heterostructures of CQDs/GQDs with ZnO nanorods (Fig. 9 a) have been studied by Ghosh et al. [85]. The I–V characteristics of ZnO-GQD (ZnO-G1, ZnO-G2) and ZnO-CQD (ZnO–C1, ZnO–C2) heterostructures in the dark and under UV (365 nm, 10 μW) light illumination conditions (Fig. 9b) revealed that the former has higher UV detectivity than the latter. The device parameters were also compared with ZnO-GO and ZnO-rGO heterostructures. ZnO-GQDs showed better UV detectivity than even ZnO-GO and ZnO-rGO (Fig. 9c). The mechanism involved in oxide-based UV detection is the photodesorption under UV illumination of the adsorbed oxygen by capturing “hot” electrons. In contrast, in ZnO-GQDs- or ZnO-CQDs-based heterostructures, the hydrophilic surface of the CQDs/GQDs largely captured oxygen moieties. Here, photoexcited electrons from GQDs and CQDs were transferred to ZnO and increased the photocurrent through suitable band alignment.

Broadband photodetectors generally demand materials with absorption and emission in the deep UV, visible, and near-infrared (NIR) regions. Single narrow bandgap semiconductors (PbS, PbSe, PbTe, HgTe, and InGaAs) [105–109] showed infrared absorption, while high bandgap semiconductors like oxides and nitrides (ZnO, GaN, and AlN) [110–112] exhibited UV and DUV absorption. CQDs/GQDs can favorably mimic the absorption in the deep UV to NIR spectral range through suitable designs using different sizes and external doping. Therefore, they can be ideal candidates for efficient and cost-effective broadband photodetectors. Tang et al. [113] used nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQD) for manipulation of the broadband response from the PDs. They adopted a facile synthesis route via microwave irradiation of glucose and aqueous ammonia to prepare N-GQDs with an emission band in the range of 300–1000 nm. The photodetectors fabricated using interdigitated gold electrodes allowed the measurement of photovoltage at a fixed current under different light illumination conditions (Fig. 10 a). The on/off response ratios at 365, 808, and 980 nm illumination were 1.1, 6.49 and 4.29, respectively (Fig. 10a). The responsivities for these illuminations were 1.14, 10.91, and 4.79 V/W, respectively, which clearly demonstrate the broadband response of the PD device.

The mechanism involved in such broadband absorption and emission can be understood through the illustration in Fig. 10b-g. The absorption of deep UV photons mainly occurs by a transition (v3---c3) in the localized π electrons present in sp2 hybridized carbon (CC) bonds. In contrast, the emission occurs through radiative recombination (c3---v3) after vibration relaxation (Fig. 10b,e). The visible light emission occurred when the excited electron underwent an interband transition (c3---c2) before radiative recombination (c2---v2) (Fig. 10e). The visible light absorption (v2---c2) took place from the partially conjugated π electrons. NIR emission was seen because of the vibration relaxation in the conjugated π electrons of layered N-GQDs (Fig. 10d,g). Very small ~3 nm N-GQDs were capable of absorbing and emitting NIR light. Kim et al. [114] also reported broadband photodetection from GQDs with higher detectivity (>1011 cm Hz1/2/W) and moderate responsivity (0.2–0.5 A/W). Zhang et al. [115] introduced a defect midgap states band (MGB) in graphene through a GQDs array structure. Now, the secondary electrons can be trapped in the MGB with a longer lifetime after the initial excitation. As a result, the corresponding holes can circulate many times in the photoconductive channel before the recombination takes place. Thus, these MGB-based PDs have a high photoconductive gain with a response in a wide spectrum range of 532 nm to 10.6 μm.

Remarkable enhancements in performance have been reported in solid-state heterostructured solar cells made of GQDs and CDs in terms of the overall efficiency and stability [116–120]. These quantum dots, when incorporated, exhibited considerable enhancements in optical absorption, sensitivity, and electron transport. Xie et al. attempted a simple heterostructure of Si NW and CDs in a core-shell design to investigate its photovoltaic properties with varying CD layer thicknesses [121]. The Si/CDs core-shell heterojunction device exhibited 9.10% power conversion efficiency under AM 1.5 irradiation. This high PCE came from enhanced optical absorption due to the NW ensemble, optimized charge carrier transport, and collection efficiency associated with the 3D NW core-shell structure. Fig. 11 a and b shows the energy band diagrams of the Si/CDs heterojunction and Au/Si NW Schottky junction. The charge carriers generated in the Si/CDs heterojunction were separated upon photoirradiation by the strong built-in-potential field. Electrons can thus be collected easily at the bottom electrode while the transport of injected electrons towards the Au electrode was hampered by the energy barrier due to band offsets. On the other hand, holes from Si can easily reach Au electrodes through the HOMO band of CDs. Therefore, the CD layer acted as an electron blocking layer to increase the open-circuit voltage of the device. On the other hand, the photogenerated electrons from the Au/Si NW Schottky junction were readily collected by Au electrodes to provide a low Voc.

A similar solar cell device design strategy a was employed by fabricating planar crystalline Si (c-Si)/GQD heterojunctions [122]. The working principle of this device is similar to that of Si NW/CD core-shell heterojunctions. The device provided a PCE of 6.63% with significant stability for more than six months. The GQD layer worked as an electron blocking medium to efficiently prevent rapid recombination of injected charge carriers. Dutta et al. also utilized GQDs to fabricate heterojunction with ZnO NWs for photovoltaic applications [123]. Vertical arrays of ZnO NWs were infiltrated and covered with GQDs synthesized by a one-step hydrazine reduction of graphene oxide. Control experiments showed that the photovoltaic effect took place due to the formation of a heterojunction between GQDs and ZnO. The device exhibited a Jsc = 0.45 mA with a Voc = 0.8 V and FF = 0.5. In a PEDOT: PSS-based PV device, electrochemically derived GQDs were used as potential electron acceptors impregnated in P3HT polymer [124]. A schematic of the fabricated device and the corresponding energy band diagram are shown in Fig. 11c and d, respectively. The HOMO level for P3HT was 5.2 eV, while the LUMO level of GQDs was in the range of 4.2–4.4 eV. Therefore, the theoretical value of Voc was estimated to be around 0.8 V, which is close to the obtained experimental value (0.77 V). At a LUMO of 3.5 V, the photogenerated electrons of P3HT could easily be injected towards the Al electrode through GQDs, while the holes from P3HT reached ITO.

Incorporation of CQDs and GQDs in specific heterostructured devices resulted in improvements in open-circuit voltage due to its band structure. This improvement can be attributed to the advanced properties of these CQDs and GQDs; that is, they are efficient photoabsorbers [26,125] and effective sensitizers [126,127]. As such, they can effectively passivate the grain boundaries of photosensitive materials [128]. CD/TiOx has been utilized as an electron transport layer in a planar heterojunction-based perovskite solar cell to improve its efficiency. An efficiency of 19% was achieved with a planar n-i-p heterojunction structure [129]. Also, CDs can effectively passivate the grain boundaries of perovskite materials by improving their environmental stability. Guo et al. have demonstrated the passivation of grain boundaries of MAPbI3 by binding with CDs [128]. Using an optimized concentration of CDs in MAPbI3, they achieved a PCE of 19.38% and long stability. Table 1 summarizes the use of CQDs and GQDs in thin-film solar cell applications.

Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are photovoltaic devices that utilize dyes to absorb sunlight. As they are solution-processed at low cost, they can conveniently be engineered through facile deposition schemes such as spin coating and inkjet printing. Incorporation of photosensitive dyes with GQDs resulted in effective solar energy harvesting materials [130–132]. Yan et al. first applied GQDs with a maximum absorbance at 591 nm in DSSCs to replace the conventional metal complexes [125]. However, a very low efficiency was obtained for the DSSC due to the weak affinity between GQDs and TiO2 surfaces. To enhance the physical adsorption and promote charge injection, nitrogen-doped CDs were employed to provide covalent attachment and to adjust the band energy structure [133]. They found a decrease in the work function of TiO2 due to the presence of nitrogen, which finally led to improvements in the device performance. Optimized doping conditions in the fabricated DSSC resulted in a conversion efficiency of 0.13%. Besides, Fang et al. and Mihalache et al. attempted to form type II band alignment in TiO2/GQDs interfaces to enhance charge collection and separation. A conversion efficiency of 6.10% was been achieved under the optimum dye concentrations [134,135].

CDs and CQDs have unique potential to replace phosphor-based white light emitting diodes. Quantum dot-based light-emitting diodes provide high efficiency, high quantum yield, better stability, and lower toxicity compared to phosphor-based LEDs that contain rare earth materials and common metal components (e.g., cadmium and lead). Motivated by these promising features, researchers have developed strategies to employ CDs and GQDs for both phosphors-based and electroluminescence-based LEDs [136–140].

Traditional phosphor-based white LEDs are known to suffer from poor stability. Luminescent carbon nanostructures have been demonstrated successfully as potential light-emitting materials. Chen et al. [141] have reported the one-step pyrolysis of N-acetylecysteine for the preparation of CDs, where they have observed broad yellow fluorescence from CDs. Further, white LEDs (WLEDs) were fabricated by combining yellow light-emitting CDs with blue GaN LEDs. With this intense combination of color between yellow and blue light, they have achieved white light as per the CEI standard. Guo et al. took an alternative approach to fabricate WLEDs by combining UV LEDs and CDs prepared from photonic crystals [142]. Although the CIE coordinates were close to white light, unwanted leakage of UV light was the major drawback of this scheme. Qu et al. used a different strategy to achieve high quantum yield and to evade the aggregation-induced quenching effect (AIQ) [143]. They prepared a composite of CDs with starch to fabricate a solid-state white LED. The WLEDs exhibited an optimized current of 50 mA and emitted white light with CIE coordinates of (0.26, 0.33). The AIQ effect is the biggest roadblock in realizing solid-state CD-based LEDs. The different types of surface functional groups attached to the CDs interact with one another during the drying process, which enhanced the AIQ effect. Therefore, the AIQ effect can be reduced by synthesizing CDs with purer surfaces. Li et al. successfully designed carbon nanorings with persistent luminescence [144]. Due to the ring-like structure, the interaction among the donor and acceptor groups on the surface was reduced considerably, resulting in better luminescence. Another CD composite-based strategy was developed by Kwon et al. Initially, CDs and GQDs were synthesized in an oil phase. Further, their composites were prepared with PMMA. Solid-state LEDs prepared in this scheme demonstrated white light luminescence when combined with InGaN blue (400 nm) LEDs [145,146].

Apart from phosphor-based WLEDs, there have been great recent improvements in GQD-based organic LEDs. Gupta et al. demonstrated an attractive way of reducing the turn-on voltage from 6 V to 4 V by combining methylene blue functionalized GQDs with MEH-PPV [129]. They also observed enhanced internal quantum efficiency with the introduction of GQDs. This enhancement may be accounted for by the higher availability of electrical transport paths due to the GQDs in the matrix. The effective electrical transport helped enhance charge injection in the LED active region, causing an increase in the carrier density. Hence, this LED device exhibited higher efficiency with a lower turn-on voltage. Sun et al. attempted to increase the work function of ITO while simultaneously reducing the surface roughness of the anode by using PEDOT: PSS as a buffer layer [147]. With this scheme, they achieved an external quantum efficiency of 0.083% at 5 mA/cm2 with CIE ordinates of (0.40, 0.43).

The size dependence emission behavior of GQDs has been demonstrated (e.g., from blue to the brown region with an increase in the size of GQDs) [148]. Kwon et al. used the GQDs as a dopant in the CBP host and achieved bright white electroluminescence for a 10 nm active layer thickness. In another interesting report, Zhang et al. showed the variation of electroluminescence from blue to white light with varying working voltages. Several attempts have been made by different groups to address the problem of low efficiency in GQD-based LEDs [149]. Further investigations are necessary to optimize the different material combinations with the GQDs/CQDs and to design better performing LED devices.

Due to their compatibility with flexible substrates processes, GQDs and CQDs have been utilized successfully in fabricating a wide range of flexible devices including: supercapacitors, photosensors, non-volatile memories, and light-emitting (or electrochromic) devices [116,150–152]. Considerable utilization of CQDs has been observed recently in advanced supercapacitor applications. Optimized anchoring of CQDs to nanostructures of conventional supercapacitor electrodes has proven to be an effective way to enhance supercapacitor performance. A comparative study on the electrochemical properties was also carried out to assess the performance differences between flexible electrodes made of MnO2 nanorods and CQD decorated MnO2 nanowires [31]. As shown in Fig. 12 , CQDs with MnO2 nanowires exhibited better stability and specific capacitance even after 1000 cycles.

Graphene-GQD chelate-based transparent supercapacitors on flexible substrates were fabricated in an attempt to realize transparent power supply units. The devices exhibited high transparency (92% at 550 nm), high energy storage efficiency (9.09 μF/cm2), very short relation time (8.55 ms) and commendable stability even after 10,000 bending test cycles [153]. Polypyrrole composite was employed with CQDs by electrochemical deposition in a different approach to synthesize electrode materials for transparent solid-state supercapacitors [154]. The flexible supercapacitors have a specific capacitance of 315 mF/cm2, with a current density of 0.2 mA/cm2 and cycling stability of 2000 [154]. An effort was made to boost the specific capacitance and energy storage density by assembling nitrogen and oxygen co-doped GQDs onto hierarchical carbon networks. The hierarchical structure had high electrical conductivity and high surface area, which led to efficient activation of GQDs with electrically conducting paths by providing a high pseudo-capacitance and electrical double layer capacitance (461 mF/cm2 at a current density of 0.5 mA/cm2) [155].

Apart from flexible supercapacitor applications, several groups have investigated the compatibility of CQDs and GQDs in flexible optoelectronic, photovoltaic, and memory devices. Non-volatile rewritable memory behavior was demonstrated for GQDs (embedded in a PMMA layer) prepared from double-walled carbon nanotubes [156]. In contrast, Lee et al. used GQDs to prepare a metal-free composite ink with PEDOT: PSS for the development of paper-based counter electrodes in flexible DSSCs [157]. Likewise, CQDs were successfully embedded into PVA polymer with submicron patterns for flexible light-emitting devices [158]. The pattering of PVA with CQDs enhanced its internal quantum yield and light extracting efficiency [158].

Production of hydrogen fuel via renewable sources has remained a challenge in meeting global energy needs. The generation of hydrogen fuel from green CQDs and GQDs is a very promising approach to provide clean energy. Elemental abundance with resistance to photobleaching makes CQDs and GQDs promising alternatives to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. The broad and tunable absorption range and high absorption coefficients make CQDs and GQDs very useful in the fabrication of photoelectrodes for photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen generation via water splitting. Several heterostructures with CQDs and GQDs have been attempted, and these had unique advantages when combined with one-dimensional nanostructures like TiO2, Si, ZnO, and Cu2S nanorods. 1D nanostructures for PEC water splitting have been produced to make heterostructures with several elements like noble metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, and Pt), and semiconductor quantum dots (CdS, CdSe, CdTe, PbS). However, heterostructures with CQDs and GQDs provided both a green route and higher conversion efficiency.

CQD-decorated heterojunctions were employed for PEC water oxidation [159]. The CDs/ZnO:Ga/ZnO heterojunction photoanode was demonstrated to improve PEC performance over ZnO:Ga/ZnO photoanodes. The CQDs/ZnO:Ga/ZnO multijunction photoanode showed a very low onset potential, a photocurrent density of ~1.5 mA cm−2 at 1.6 V vs. RHE and good photo-stability. Yu et al. [160] also reported TiO2/CQDs heterostructures for PEC with an optimized photocurrent of 1.2 mA cm−2 at 0 V versus Ag/AgCl under 100 mW cm−2 simulated sunlight. Liang et al. [161] showed TiO2/CQDs photoanodes with a photocurrent density of ~3.0 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under simulated sunlight. Ye et al. [162] introduced CQDs in a BiVO4-based integrated photoanode (Fig. 13 a) with an ultrathin interlayer of isolated CQDs between the BiVO4 core and the two OEC shells (NiOOH/FeOOH). BiVO4 has absorption in the visible region, while CQDs possess broadband absorption from UV to Visible to NIR. Therefore, the insertion of CQDs both enhances the absorption of the solar spectrum and acts as an efficient electron transfer medium to the respective Pt cathode for HER (Fig. 13b). In our recent study we demonstrate a multifacet Si nanowire (SiNW) decorated with surface plasmon-enhanced carbon quantum dots (AuCQDs) as efficient, stable, economical, and scalable photocathodes (PCs) for HER. The PEC performance of SiNW_AuCQDs showed more than a four fold efficiency enhancement than the pristine SiNW which was attributed to the combined effect of enhanced solar absorption and carrier transport. The optimized PC SiNW_AuCQDs results in the highest photocurrent ~1.7 mA/cm2, an applied bias photon-to-current conversion efficiency of ~0.8%, and H2 gas evolution rate of ~182.93 μmol h−1 [163].

On the other hand, Zeng et al. [164] reported the unique layer-by-layer assembly of nitrogen-doped GQD monolayers on 1D ZnO nanorods for PEC water splitting (Fig. 14 ). In layer-by-layer growth, the intrinsic negatively charged surface of hydrophilic N-GQDs was the deciding factor for the growth on hydrophilic ZnO nanowire surfaces. The optimum conversion efficiency (i.e., IPCE) for the ZnO/N-GQD was ~0.29%, which is 10 times higher than that of only ZnO. Xu et al. [165] also reported GQDs-decorated 3D periodic multiscale TiO2 architectures for PEC water splitting. The optimum photocurrent density was ~2.92 mA cm−2 under simulated one-sun illumination with 72% IPCE at a 350 nm wavelength.

A comparison of the performances of different CQD/GQD-based devices for different applications is provided in Tables (1–3
). CQDs/GQDs have an edge over other materials in photodetection, especially UV detection. Monolayer GQDs have shown a responsivity of ~2.1 mA/W [95], whereas GQD decorated ZnO nanorods on GaN showed a responsivity of ~35 mA/W [24]. In the case of thin-film heterojunction solar cells, organic-halide perovskite has had a tremendous impact on the photovoltaic research with an efficiency of 23%. However, the presence of large-trap states at the TiO2/perovskite interface does not allow efficient electron transport. In contrast, CQDs exhibit high electron mobility with very low surface trap states. Therefore, a combination of CQDs/TiO2 can synergistically increase both the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density in perovskite solar cells with a maximum power conversion efficiency of ~19% [166]. GQDs also show promise as electron transporting layers in perovskite-based devices. Zhang et al. reported efficiency enhancement from 17% to 18.9% when GQDs was incorporated in the perovskite solar cells [167]. Quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) have shown huge potential, approaching an efficiency of ~13%. The main advantage of QDSSC is the easy low-cost production of quantum dots (QDs) and solution processability. Further, the size tunability of these QDs based on diverse synthesis approaches gives them unique optical properties. However, most of the inorganic QDs used in QDSSC suffer from stability problems in electrolytic conditions. After the discovery of CQDs/GQDs, they were often employed as sensitizers in QDSSC. The success of such applications was comparable to inorganic semiconductor QDs. However, due to the low absorption in the solar spectrum, there is still a lot of room for further improvement. Riaz et al. reported 8.2% efficiency using nitrogen-doped CQDs as sensitizers [168]. These emerging CQDs/GQDs with high cost-effectiveness and low toxicity appear to be an ideal replacement for semiconductor QDs in LED devices due to their tunable stable fluorescence emission and facile synthesis. Attempts have been made to improve the LED performance by modifying existing heterostructures with CQDs/GQDs in the active region [141,142]. However, efforts are ongoing to use the full potential of these CQDs/FQDs with the combination of the right materials to acquire the optimal LED in terms of electroluminescent efficiency. For the generation of hydrogen, CQDs/GQDs have been made as heterostructures with well-established semiconductor photoelectrodes like ZnO, TiO2, BiVO4, and Cu2S. The reported solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiency of these CQD/GQD-based photoelectrodes did not reach ~2–3% [161,164]. The targeted efficiency of these CQD/GQD-based PEs are much lower than tandem semiconductor photoelectrodes (STH ~10%). The cost and stability are still obstacles to the large-scale production of these tandem-based photoelectrodes due to their sophisticated designs. However, the use of emerging photoelectrodes based on CQD/GQD heterostructures is facile and cost-effective, providing enhanced potential for large-scale production.

In conclusion, we have summarized the advantages of CQDs/GQDs and their energy applications like solar cells and PEC water splitting, and optoelectronic devices like LEDs, photodetectors, and flexible electronics. Both GQDs and CQDs have attracted tremendous research interest, especially in the energy sector. The references in this review were largely published the last 5–6 years. Based on the various advanced properties of CQDs/GQDs, significant improvements are expected to be made in the existing device design to further expand their applications. Although both CQDs/GQDs have attracted tremendous research interest, the development of efficient and stable prototypes for the above-discussed applications from CQDs/GQDs is still far away.

In CQDs/GQDs based hetero structure detector, asymmetric electrode plays a vital role in modulating photogenerated electron-hole flow. In contrast, a broadband detector was demonstrated by the vibration relaxation in the conjugated π electrons of layered N-GQDs. Functionalized CQDs/GQDs exhibited electron transporting or blocking layer in heterostructure solar cell. Luminescent CQDs/GQDs have been demonstrated successfully as potential light-emitting materials with variable color from blue to brown. CQDs have been embedded into polymer with submicron patterns for flexible light-emitting devices. CQD-decorated heterojunctions were also employed for PEC water splitting mainly by surface passivation with heterostructure photoelectrode.

As optical properties of the CQDs/GQDs are not well understood, more efforts are needed to gain solid experimental evidence for their characterization (e.g., the effect of size, surface chemistry, and doping in CQDs/GQDs). Further, explicit analysis is demanded to understand the interaction of mostly used hybrid structure (CQDs/GQDs) with other functional counterparts. The presence of organic moieties over CQDs surfaces is a major issue (e.g., non-radiative recombination and bonding with the surrounding atoms) in electronic applications like photodetectors or electronic sensors. In contrast, GQDs have the advantages of being highly conducting graphene-like structures, while they suffer from low PL quantum yield. Fluorescence intensity is very important for a luminescence-based sensor for the sensitive detection of targets. Therefore, the synthesis technique has to be modified to prepare high PL QY within the framework of a top-down approach. The surface defects must be minimized as recombination will otherwise take away the advantages. We have also discussed the current status of CQD/GQD-based flexible electronics, which is an emerging research field. Further, the use of these CQDs/GQDs to modify the existing photoelectrodes for PEC water splitting was also summarized in the current context. Although the success of these CQD/GQD-based photoelectrodes is still limited, they have enormous potential for improvement to further expand their applications in various fields.
